coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of efficient interface carrier channels on multidimensional OCQD Decorated CN Heterojunction for tetracycline degradation via peroxymonosulfate activation

coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Xusheng
Zhang, Dehui
Yu, Chengmin
Su, Weiyan
Wu, Guangyu
Xing, Weinan
coredata.dc:description: Graphite carbon nitride (CN) is recognized as a promising non-metallic semiconductor photocatalyst for its excellent chemical stability and visible light response. However, the high electron-hole recombination rate in CN greatly limits its photocatalytic performance. To circumvent these limitations, it is particularly important to explore an efficient way to promote the migration of carriers in CN. This study innovatively designs a multidimensional heterojunction between organic carbon quantum (OCQD) and CN, which named as OCQD-CN. OCQD acts as an carriers transfer channel, which effectively promotes the separation efficiency of carriers. Meanwhile, the introduction of OCQD further improves light absorption scope as well as light reaction intensity of CN. By adding peroxymonosulfate (PMS) to form an advanced oxidation process, OCQD-CN10 exhibits a prominent tetracycline (TC) degradation capacity up to 89.1 % within 60 min. The photocatalytic degradation performance of OCQD-CN is significantly improved by 2.0 times with the addition of OCQD. The first-order rate constant corresponding of OCQD-CN10 (0.0300 min−1) is also 2.8 times higher than CN (0.0107 min−1). Thereby, this research underscores the potential of OCQD-CN/PMS system in mitigating water pollution and advancing eco-friendly technologies, offering a promising route to address antibiotic contaminants in the environment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalysis
Tetracycline
Organic carbon quantum dots
Graphite carbon nitride

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photocatalytic degradation of TC by activating PMS
Effects of OCQDs contents on TC degradation in O-CQD-CN/PMS system
Effects of catalyst dosage on TC degradation in O-CQD-CN/PMS system
Impact of PMS dosage on TC degradation in O-CQD-CN/PMS system
Effects of pH on TC degradation in O-CQD-CN/PMS system
Effects of initial TC concentration on TC degradation in O-CQD-CN/PMS system

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a-b) SEM images of OCQD-CN10, (c-d) TEM images of OCQD-CN10.
(a) XRD patterns of CN, OCQD and different proportions of OCQD-CN, (b) FTIR spectra of CN and different proportions of OCQD-CN.
XPS spectra of CN, OCQD and OCQD-CN10: (a) C 1 s, (b) N 1 s, (c) O 1 s, (d) S 2p.
(a) UV–Visible diffuse reflectance spectra, (b) Band gap energy plots of CN and different proportions of OCQD-CN10, (c) XPS VB spectra, (d) Electronic band structure for CN and OCQD-CN10.
(a-b) Effect of OCQD ratio on degradation of TC, (c-d) Effect of catalyst dosage on degradation of TC.
(a-b) Effect of PMS dosage on TC degradation, (c-d) Impact of pH levels on TC degradation efficiency.
(a-b) Effect of TC concentration on degradation performance.
Cyclic experiments of OCQD-CN10 on TC.
(a) Photocurrent response curve, (b) EIS spectra, (c) PL spectra of CN and OCQD-CN10.
(a-b) Effect of different quenchers on degradation performance.
Diagram showing the reaction mechanism of the OCQD-CN10/PMS/Vis system.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of efficient interface carrier channels on multidimensional OCQD Decorated CN Heterojunction for tetracycline degradation via peroxymonosulfate activation

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: dNational Positioning Observation Station of Hung-tse Lake Wetland Ecosystem in Jiangsu Province, Hongze 223100, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphite carbon nitride (CN) is recognized as a promising non-metallic semiconductor photocatalyst for its excellent chemical stability and visible light response. However, the high electron-hole recombination rate in CN greatly limits its photocatalytic performance. To circumvent these limitations, it is particularly important to explore an efficient way to promote the migration of carriers in CN. This study innovatively designs a multidimensional heterojunction between organic carbon quantum (OCQD) and CN, which named as OCQD-CN. OCQD acts as an carriers transfer channel, which effectively promotes the separation efficiency of carriers. Meanwhile, the introduction of OCQD further improves light absorption scope as well as light reaction intensity of CN. By adding peroxymonosulfate (PMS) to form an advanced oxidation process, OCQD-CN10 exhibits a prominent tetracycline (TC) degradation capacity up to 89.1 % within 60 min. The photocatalytic degradation performance of OCQD-CN is significantly improved by 2.0 times with the addition of OCQD. The first-order rate constant corresponding of OCQD-CN10 (0.0300 min−1) is also 2.8 times higher than CN (0.0107 min−1). Thereby, this research underscores the potential of OCQD-CN/PMS system in mitigating water pollution and advancing eco-friendly technologies, offering a promising route to address antibiotic contaminants in the environment.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As a widely used antibiotic, the overuse and improper discharge of tetracycline (TC) hydrochloride has profound environmental implications [1,2]. In aquatic environments, TC exhibits high stability and bioaccumulation [3,4]. The prolonged presence of TC in the environment may lead to increased microbial resistance, disrupting the balance of aquatic ecosystems and potentially posing threats to human health, such as triggering allergic reactions and causing liver and kidney damage [5]. The current methods for treating wastewater containing TC mainly include physical, chemical, and biological approaches [6-9]. Among these methods, advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) such as persulfate oxidation and ozonation have attracted extensive attention in the field of water treatment due to their simple process and low cost [10,11]. Photocatalysis is an advanced technology that produces reactive oxygen species under illumination [12,13]. The use of semiconductor materials such as titanium dioxide (TiO2) for photocatalytic processes has also received increasing attention [14-16]. High-energy photons could stimulate electrons on the surface of semiconductors, thereby generating reactive oxygen species (ROS) [17]. These ROS could effectively degrade TC molecules, breaking them down into harmless end products, making it a green, economical, and safe advanced oxidation process [18]. Photocatalytic technology is influenced by light conditions and material stability, to enhance water treatment efficiency [19,20], researchers have initiated investigations into integrating it with other advanced oxidation processes [21]. Among these, photocatalytic peroxymonosulfate (PMS) degradation technology stands out as a notable developmental pathway [22]. This method combines photocatalysis with persulfate oxidation technology, showing promise for effectively degrading organic pollutants in water [23,24]. Therefore, it is particularly meaningful to find an excellent photocatalyst to activate PMS to degrade pollutants.

The current mainstream method involves constructing a metal/PMS collaborative system [25,26]. However, after the reaction, due to the leaching of metal ions and the change of their valence, it may cause secondary pollution to the environment and could not be reused many times [27]. Graphitic carbon nitride (CN) is a non-metallic photocatalytic material with good redox ability, excellent chemical stability and thermal stability [28,29]. The triazine ring structure and coordination N atoms in CN could provide electronic sites for the OO bond cleavage of PMS, thus generate ROS to participate in the reaction [30,31]. However, CN also encounters the disadvantages of insufficient light absorption capacity, slow electron migration rate and migration direction disorder [32,33]. In order to break through the photocatalytic performance limitations caused by these shortcomings, it would be a fascinating idea to construct a new type of CN from a multidimensional perspective.

As a traditional two-dimensional material, CN is considered one of the ideal catalysts for photocatalytic degradation of TC due to its non-metallic properties and suitable band structure [34,35]. However, single-component CN will inevitably face the high electron-hole recombination rate, thus it is particularly attractive to use two compounds to take advantage of both materials and overcome their shortcomings [36]. Zero-dimensional (0D) quantum dots could be uniformly dispersed on two-dimensional (2D) nanosheets, thereby improving their photocatalytic efficiency by promoting the transfer of electrons [37]. However, compared to 2D structures, three-dimensional (3D) frameworks provide a richer set of surface-active sites that could be better combined with 0D quantum dots [38]. At the same time, the porous structure of the 3D flower-like CN provides abundant channels for carrier's transfer, which could promote separation of photogenerated carriers. By forming a heterojunction structure between 0D quantum dot and 3D CN, photogenerated carriers could be efficiently dissociated, thereby promoting the redox reaction. Researchers are exploring the combination of 3D CN and 0D semiconductor to further improve the photocatalytic performance. Liu et al. developed a novel composite material by creating a 3D porous CN and Fe2O3 quantum dot structure [39]. This composite exhibited high degradation rate of various organic pollutants through synergistic effects of photocatalysis as well as Fenton effect. Huang et al. fabricated a Pd/SCN composite material by integrating Pd nanoparticles into a 3D hollow spherical structure of CN [40]. This composite demonstrated prominent performance in hydrogen evolution. Nguyen et al. synthesized a Z scheme catalyst, NiCo2O4@CN, by combining 0D/3D materials [23]. This photocatalyst could degrade ibuprofen by 96 % in 60 min. However, metal quantum dots may lead to secondary environmental pollution due to leaching of metal ions, so it is particularly important to seek a new type of non-metal quantum dots.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a typical 0D material, have shown significant advantages in the field of photocatalytic degradation due to their wide spectral absorption, rich active sites brought by high specific surface area etc. [41,42]. Compared with traditional CQDs, the organic carbon quantum dots (OCQDs) have higher selectivity, which may bring more possibilities in improving the photocatalytic performance. The similar elemental composition and non-metallic properties of CN and OCQDs could make them well matched and have a high degree of stability. However, there are few studies based on the novel OCQDs to form 0D/3D heterojunctions with CN to enhance their photocatalytic activity for high degradation of TC by activating PMS.

Herein, a novel 0D OCQD-modified 3D flower-like CN heterojunction to enhance separation efficiency of photo-generated carriers and achieve efficient activation of PMS to degrade TC is proposed. The addition of OCQD makes OCQD-CN enhance light absorption capacity and promote the separation of carriers, thereby generating more free electrons and holes to participate in the reaction. After adding OCQDs, the degradation rate of TC could reach 89.1 % in 60 min, which is 2.0 times higher than CN. The mechanism is further clarified by adjusting the PMS dosage, pH and free radical trapping experiments. This work designs a novel non-metallic heterojunction material to promote the separation of photo-generated carriers and broaden prospects for environmental applications.

1.0 g of melamine and 1.0 g of cyanuric acid were fully ground in a mortar separately and dissolved in 40 ml and 20 ml of dimethyl sulfoxide, respectively. After the two solutions were completely dissolved to clear and transparent, the cyanuric acid mixture was slowly added to the melamine mixture under stirring conditions, and the milky white suspension was formed by continuous stirring for 30 min. After that, the suspension was filtered with ethanol and dried to obtain CN precursors. Finally, the CN precursor was heated at 500 °C in a muffle furnace for 2 h to obtain 3D CN.

2.0 g of citric acid hydrate and 1.0 g of cysteamine hydrochloride were dissolved in 5 mL deionized water. The solution was then evaporated in an oven at 80 °C for 12 h to yield a syrup. This syrup was transferred to a high-pressure stainless-steel reactor lined with polytetrafluoroethylene and heated at 200 °C for 3.5 h. The solid product was collected, thoroughly washed with deionized water, and dried at 60 °C to obtain a brown powder, which is identified as OCQDs.

In an agate mortar, 1.0 g of the CN precursor was combined with 5 mg, 10 mg, and 20 mg of OCQDs, respectively. The mixture was thoroughly ground for 10–20 min. Subsequently, the combined sample was subjected to thermal polymerization by heating at 500 °C for 2 h in a muffle furnace. After the heating process, the solid product was allowed to cool naturally to room temperature, resulting in the formation of OCQD-CN5, OCQD-CN10, and OCQD-CN20.

The morphology characteristics of OCQD-CN10 are investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) as well as transmission electron microscopy (TEM). From Fig. 1
a, it could be seen that OCQD-CN10 is shaped like a 3D flower-like ball. Fig. 1 b shows the enlarged SEM image, displaying that the size of the 3D flower ball is in the range of 1–2 μm. In Fig. 1 c, OCQD-CN10 is a sample consisting of loose nanoflakes, with a loose and porous texture and some folds at the edges. Fig. 1 d shows that some OCQDs are dispersed on the CN surface. The average diameter of these OCQDs is <10 nm, which is beneficial for shortening the carrier migration distance. It could also be seen that many isolated and dispersed dark spots are deposited on the CN surface, indicating a prominent interfacial interaction between CN and OCQDs. The above indicates that OCQDs have been successfully formed a heterojunction with CN through close interfacial contact.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) shows crystal structure of OCQD-CN heterojunction photocatalyst. As could be seen from Fig. 2
a, all samples have diffraction peaks at 13.3° and 27.2°, which belong to (100) and (002) planes [43]. These peak characteristics clearly show the ordered stacking of interlayer cyclic structures and the periodic arrangement of tri-s-triazine units in plane [44]. Experimental results indicate that introduction of O-CQDs leads to the significant decrease in typical peak intensity of CN host material, which is attributed to decrease the crystallinity and stacking order of the triazine units within CN [45]. However, the characteristic peaks represented by OCQD are not reflected in OCQD-CN, which may be due to the low content of OCQD or the lattice plane of OCQD is encapsulated in CN [46,47]. In addition, the XRD patterns of OCQD-CN composite materials exhibit a high degree of similarity to CN, suggesting that the binding process of O-CQDs does not cause significant disturbance to the original structural framework of CN [48].

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra is carried out to explore functional group structure. The peak at 808 cm−1 is related to triazine ring unit, while the peaks around 1200–1700 cm−1 are the aromatic carbon-nitrogen heterocyclic ring [41]. All samples have wide signal peaks between 3040 and 3520 cm−1, which are related to adsorbed water molecules or tert butyl amino groups [49]. The modified material reveals a new peak at 3432 cm−1, indicating that OCQDs have been successfully incorporated into CN. The FT-IR spectra of OCQD-CN5, OCQD-CN10, and OCQD-CN20 show the similar characteristic bands as the CN control, indicating that CN retains its original structure after the introduction of OCQDs.

In the analysis of heterojunctions, the movement of electrons is often a key link. When heterojunctions are formed, electrons will migrate from material with higher Fermi levels to material with lower Fermi levels [50]. The material loses electrons will be positively charged, and the one has gained electrons will be negatively charged, thus forming a built-in electric field. After that, due to influence of electron movement, there will be some degree of change of the binding energy [51]. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) could illustrate flow direction of electrons between materials by the change of the binding energy, and it is also used to analyze bonding on the photocatalyst surface. As shown in Fig. S1, all materials contain a large amount of non-metallic elements C, N, O. The XPS spectrum of C 1 s (Fig. 3
a) divides into two peaks at 284.6 eV and 288.1 eV, which are respectively correlated with CC and sp2 hybridized carbon (NC = N) [52]. The peak of the graphitic carbon component of OCQD-CN10 is significantly higher than that of CN due to the addition of OCQD, which has a similar elemental composition to CN. The N 1 s spectra in Fig. 3 b could be subdivided into four peaks, including N (C = NC) at 398.4 eV, N (N-(C)3) at 400.0 eV, CN-H at 401.3 eV, and π excitation at 404.2 eV [53]. The O 1 s spectra is shown in Fig. 3 c, the peak at 532.3 eV originates from surface adsorbed water, and a new peak at 531.6 eV is detected in OCQD-CN10, which is related to oxygen doping that inevitably occurs during thermal polymerization in air conditions [54]. The S 2p spectrum is shown in Fig. 3 d, the peak at 163.5 eV of OCQD is corresponding to C-S-C bond, and after OCQD and CN form a composite structure, the peak has a negative shift. Compared with CN, binding energies of C, N, O in OCQD-CN10 all have a certain degree of negative shift. While for OCQD, the binding energy of S has a positive shift in comparison with OCQD-10. This opposite change in binding energy indicates formation of a built-in electric field [55].

UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) is measured to explore the light-absorbing properties of materials. Fig. 4
a shows the optical absorption edge of CN is around 480 nm. After introduction of OCQDs, the absorption edge of OCQD-CN gradually redshifts with the increase of OCQDs content, and optical absorption intensity gradually increases. According to Fig. 4 b, the calculated bandgaps (Eg) of OCQD-CN5, OCQD-CN10, and OCQD-CN20 are 2.36 eV, 2.67 eV, 2.70 eV. The OCQD-CN heterojunction material has narrower band gap than CN, which makes it easier for electrons to transfer from valence band (VB) to conduction band (CB) [56], resulting in the generation of more photogenerated carriers under visible light, thereby improving catalytic performance. The semiconductor type and flat band potential (Ef) are determined through Mott-Schottky (MS) electrochemical testing, as shown in Fig. S2. The Ef values obtained from Ag/AgCl for CN and OCQD-CN10 are −0.27 V and −0.36 V respectively, indicating CN and OCQD-CN10 are both n-type semiconductors.

The relationship between Ef and ERHE could be expressed as ERHE = EAg/AgCl +0.198 eV+0.0592 pH [57], revealing Ef values of 0.33 eV and 0.24 eV for CN and OCQD-CN with respect to RHE. XPS VB spectrum in Fig. 4 c shows a 0.71 eV relative potential between VB and Ef of CN. The addition of OCQDs results in a significant decrease in the VB value. The CB values for CN and OCQD-CN are −1.84 eV and −1.57 eV (Fig. 4 d). Overall, the incorporation of OCQDs effectively leads to band gap narrowing, enhancing photoelectron transition efficiency, and improving photocatalytic performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation of TC by activating PMS
ce:section.ce:para: In order to systematically evaluate the activity of the catalyst in the photocatalytic degradation process and to accurately optimize various reaction conditions, this study focuses on investigating the comprehensive impact of multiple key variables, such as catalyst dosage, PMS addition, and initial pH value influence the breakdown effectiveness of TC in the OCQD-CN/PMS system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of OCQDs contents on TC degradation in O-CQD-CN/PMS system
ce:section.ce:para:
In addition, OCQD-CN is modified with different OCQDs contents. Fig. 5
a-b illustrates that OCQD-CN10′s degradation rate attains as much as 85.0 %, with a reaction rate constant of 0.0300 min−1 (Fig. S3). Nonetheless, degradation rates of OCQD-CN5 and OCQD-CN20 are 64.9 % and 76.1 %, with reaction rate constants of 0.0148 min−1 and 0.0233 min−1, falling below those of OCQD-CN10. The reason for the low performance of OCQD-CN5 may be that the number of OCQDs is insufficient, and it does not produce enough carrier channels to supply the large number of carriers that are generated by light. Meanwhile, excess OCQDs will occupy the surface active center of CN and compete with PMS, resulting in the lack of surface reaction sites for PMS and inhibition of the reaction [48]. Therefore, in subsequent experiments, OCQD-CN10 should be selected as the optimal amount of photocatalyst.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of catalyst dosage on TC degradation in O-CQD-CN/PMS system
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 5 c-d, with increase of catalyst dosage, the removal of TC is improved. As the dosage of the catalyst escalates from 0 g/L to 0.4 g/L, there's a notable rise in the rate of TC degradation. The rate constants for catalyst concentrations of 0 g/L, 0.1 g/L, 0.2 g/L, 0.3 g/L, and 0.4 g/L are 0.0072 min−1, 0.0128 min−1, 0.0298 min−1, 0.0183 min−1, 0.0199 min−1 (Fig. S3). With a catalyst concentration of 0.2 g/L, degradation removal rate of TC reaches the maximum in 60 min, which is approximately 85.0 %. As the catalyst dosage increases from 0.2 g/L to 0.4 g/L, degradation efficiency of TC significantly decreases. For a certain amount of PMS, saturated active sites on catalyst surface are redundant. The findings suggest that catalyst quantity plays a crucial role in degradation. This may be because more catalysts provide more reactive sites, which favors PMS activation and thus produces more ROS to participate in the degradation reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of PMS dosage on TC degradation in O-CQD-CN/PMS system
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 6
a-b illustrate a gradual enhancement in TC's degradation efficiency as PMS increases. At a PMS dosage of 0 g/L, removal rate of TC in 60 min is only 48.5 % (Fig. 6 a). The inclusion of PMS significantly enhances the efficiency of TC's degradation. When PMS dosage is 0.3 g/L, it has the highest removal rate of 89.1 %, which is 1.83 times higher than pure catalyst. This proves that the synergy between the catalyst and PMS allows TC to be efficiently degraded. However, due to the limitation of surface-active site, the removal rate decreases while PMS is further increased. Reaction points on catalyst may quickly trigger small amounts of PMS, leading to the formation of ROS, but too much PMS needs to compete for the active site of the catalyst, which reduces the reaction rate [58].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of pH on TC degradation in O-CQD-CN/PMS system
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6 c-d show the effect of pH on TC degradation. When the pH is 3, the acidic conditions cause an imbalance in the protonation of the key substance involved in the reaction, namely, superoxide radicals (·O2 −). This imbalance results in the depletion and instability of ·O2 −, significantly inhibiting the degradation of TC [59]. As the pH value increases from 3 to 5, the apparent rate constant also improves accordingly (Fig. S4). At pH=5, the removal rate of TC reaches a maximum value (89.1 %). Further increasing the pH to 7 and 9 do not significantly increase the removal of TC. When the pH value increases to 9, there's a notable reduction in both apparent rate constant as well as efficiency of removal rate. This is primarily attributed to the catalyst's structural instability at higher pH levels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of initial TC concentration on TC degradation in O-CQD-CN/PMS system
ce:section.ce:para: Additionally, the effects of TC degradation at different concentrations are also explored. The OCQD-CN/PMS system demonstrates effective elimination efficiency across various TC concentrations. For TC levels of 5, 10, and 20 mg/L, the removal rate of TC stands at 89.1 %, 85.0 %, and 78.6 %. As the TC concentration rises, both the degradation rate and efficiency decrease. This might be due to the elevated TC concentration necessitating the decomposition of a greater number of TC molecules [60]. However, the catalyst surface's active sites are limited, which hinders the production of hydroxyl radicals. This limitation leads to insufficient free radicals in the reaction system to efficiently combat the excess TC [61]. (Fig. 7 )

At last, the stability performance and practical application prospect of OCQD-CN, cycle experiments are carried out. As shown in Fig. 8 , OCQD-CN10 maintains prominent stability in the four cycles, and the performance is only 4.2 % lower than that of the first experiment, reflecting the better stability and reusability.

The interface charge separation and migration behavior of the catalyst are investigated through photocurrent response and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Fig. 9
a shows photocurrent response of CN and OCQD-CN10 under illumination conditions. Compared with CN, the transient current response intensity of OCQD-CN10 significantly increases and remains stable during each on-off cycle. When OCQDs are anchored, the photocurrent density of OCQD-CN10 increases, separation efficiency of the carriers is significantly improved. In addition, the charge migration process is further investigated by EIS. Fig. 9 b is the Nyquist plot obtained from the test. In general, the smaller radius of the curve, the smaller resistance to charge transfer [62]. Compared with CN, OCQD-CN10 has a smaller EIS arc radius, this further demonstrates the inhibitory effect of introducing OCQDs into the catalyst on carrier recombination. In a nutshell, the formation of OCQDs and CN heterojunction significantly promotes the separation of carriers within OCQD-CN by forming a high-speed carrier channel.

Photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL) directly reflects recombination rate of electrons and holes in CN/OCQD-CN10 systems. Lower PL intensity indicates reduced electron-hole recombination and faster charge transfer [63]. CN displays a pronounced emission peak between 400 nm and 800 nm, with peak intensity around 480 nm. The PL intensity decreases notably with the addition of OCQDs, which corresponds to the data from the UV absorption edge. It means that the addition of OCQD significantly reduces the carrier recombination efficiency of OCQD-CN to generate more free electrons and holes. Therefore, construction of 0D/3D CN heterojunction is helpful to generate more free charges and holes to participate in the redox reaction, which in turn promotes the degradation of TC by activating PMS.

In order to determine the free radicals involved in OCQD/CN/PMS system, effect of different capture reagents is tested (Fig. 10 ). Quenching experiments are conducted by adding methanol (MeOH), tert‑Butanol (TBA), l-histidine (L-his), and benzoquinone (BQ) to the reaction solution [64]. As shown in Fig. 10 a, excessive BQ shows a strong inhibitory effect on TC degradation, decreasing from 89.1 % to 50.4 %. This indicates that ·O2 − plays the most important part in TC removal. While l-his, TBA and MeOH are added, removal efficiency of TC decreases to 63.4 %, 69.4 %, and 73 %. While the corresponding degradation rate constant also decreases (Fig. S5). This phenomenon indicates sulfate radicals (SO4 ·−), hydroxyl radicals (·OH), single oxygen (1O2) are also involved in removal process of TC. The order of their contribution is: ·O2 −>1O2>·OH>SO4 ·−.

Based on the above observations, this study deeply explores mechanism of photocatalytic degradation of TC by OCQD-CN heterojunction (Scheme 1 ). The introduction of OCQDs significantly enhance visible light absorption efficiency of photocatalysts, effectively promoting separation of carriers. Under the irradiation of sunlight, a large number of electron-hole pairs are rapidly formed on CN. Due to the formation of heterojunctions between CN and OCQD, which effectively promote the carrier separation and transfer. The photo-generated electrons react with PMS to generate more SO4 ·− and ·OH [65]. While photo-generated holes oxidize PMS to SO5 ·−, which then reacts with H2O to generate 1O2 and ·O2 − [56,66]. These free radicals cooperate to degrade TC molecules, ultimately producing harmless small molecules. OCQD-CN heterojunction generates more free electrons and holes to participate in the reaction, thus greatly enhancing photocatalytic activity.

In this study, a 0D/3D OCQD modified CN heterojunction photocatalyst is successfully synthesized and applied to the photocatalytic TC removal by activating PMS. In aqueous solution, the TC degradation ability of OCQD-CN is significantly improved compared with CN, and the degradation rate of OCQD-CN10 for TC at 60 min reached 89.1 %, which is twice that of CN. According to the test analysis, it could be seen that the addition of OCQD broadens the visible light absorption range of CN, so that OCQD-CN can obtain more light energy to participate in the reaction. On the other hand, OCQD forms high-speed carriers transfer channels, which promote the separation of electron-hole pairs, thus more free electrons and holes are involved in the generation of ROS, which is conducive to removal of TC with PMS. Through the pH and pollutant concentration adjustment experiments, the OCQD-CN/PMS system could show prominent TC degradation ability in complex environments. In the cyclability experiments, the degradation ability of the OCQD-CN/PMS system is almost unchanged in multiple cycles, which is attributed to the similar chemical composition of CN to OCQD and the properties of non-metals. Free radical trapping experiments confirmed ·O2 − plays the most important part in OCQD-CN/PMS system. Results reveal that OCQD-modified CN photocatalysts excel in degradation performance, holding great potential for industrial wastewater treatment as a novel, efficient technology.

Xusheng Xu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Dehui Zhang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation. Chengmin Yu: Methodology, Data curation. Weiyan Su: Formal analysis. Guangyu Wu: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Weinan Xing: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by projects of National Natural Science Foundation of China (22478195, 21901119) and the Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYCX24_1360).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.surfin.2024.105231.
